title: Right side view of Binary tree

content.problemStatement:
Given the rootrootroot of a binary tree, imagine standing on the **right side** of the tree. Return an array of the values of the nodes you can see ordered from top to bottom.

**Notes:**


Return the rightmost node value at each depth level (top → bottom).
Expected solutions commonly use BFS level-order traversal or DFS with depth tracking.
Function Declaration

Function Name



rightSideViewrightSideViewrightSideView — This function returns the list of node values visible when viewing a binary tree from the right side.



Parameters



rootrootroot: A pointer to the root node of the binary tree.
The binary tree may contain 000 to 100100100 nodes, and each node contains an integer value or may be null (`-1` in level-order input).



Return Value



Returns an array containing the values of the rightmost nodes at each depth level of the binary tree.
The values are ordered from **top to bottom**, representing the nodes visible from the right side.
constraints: Level-order input may contain `-1` indicating a null node.
The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of **two lines of input**:
The first line contains a single integer NNN - size of the array that contains nodes and empty leaf nodes.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the **level-order traversal** of the tree, where
For each test case, output on a new line the **right side view** of the binary tree —
a sequence of space-separated integers representing the values of the nodes visible from the right side.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For the first test case:

    10
   /  \
  5    15
 / \     \
3   7     20

From the right side you see 10, then 15, then 20.
